evals.prompt: I run an education content site about CNA training. I want to diversify its backlink profile with directory and profile links. Build me a plan.
evals.expected_output: Should check for product-marketing.md first. Should run the readiness check (site live/indexed, brand kit, dedicated email alias, address decision, DR/referring-domain baseline). Should run Step 2 audit of existing profiles/listings before creating anything new. Should recommend tiers 1-6 (identity profiles, web directories, content profiles, blog directories, bookmarking, niche) and note Tier 7 local layer only applies with a real physical address. Should set honest expectations: these are foundation links for diversity, mostly nofollow, not a rankings silver bullet. Should propose weekly batches of 5-10 (velocity pacing), not a one-day blast. Should reference references/directory-list.md and the tracker CSV. Should set the KPI baseline and 90-day targets (referring domains, link-type mix, listings verified).
assertions: Checks for product-marketing.md
Runs readiness check including baseline DR/referring domains
Audits existing profiles/listings before creating new
Recommends tiers 1-6 and gates Tier 7 on a real address
Sets honest expectations about link value
Paces submissions in weekly batches rather than one blast
References directory-list.md or the tracker
Sets KPI baseline and 90-day targets

evals.prompt: Can I just paste the same 150-word description into every directory and profile?
evals.expected_output: Should refuse and explain Rule 3: brand facts (name, URL, category) stay byte-for-byte identical, but descriptions vary per surface. Should explain search and AI engines discount duplicated boilerplate. Should point to the variation rules by surface type (identity profiles = factual, web directories = category-first, content platforms = editorial voice, bookmarking = page-first). Should mention the description ladder (tagline / 60-char / 150-word / 300-word) and tag rotation (pool of 12-15, use 5-8, never the identical set twice in a tier). Should reference references/positioning-variations.md.
assertions: Refuses the request
Distinguishes identical brand facts from varied descriptions
Notes duplicate boilerplate is discounted
Lists variation rules by surface type
Mentions the description ladder and tag rotation
References positioning-variations.md

evals.prompt: Should I buy one of those $99 services that submits my site to 500 directories?
evals.expected_output: Should say no, citing the rule against paid mass-submission packages. Should explain they hit spam directories (DR under 10, unmaintained, link farms) that dilute the link profile and can trigger spam classification. Should explain the platforms that matter are free and take minutes each, and the point is a curated, verified set — quality bar of DR ~40+ or genuine niche relevance. Should recommend the manual/agent-assisted batch workflow with the tracker instead.
assertions: Refuses the service
Cites the no-mass-submission rule
Warns about spam directories and dilution
States the quality bar (DR ~40+ or niche-relevant)
Recommends the curated batch workflow with tracker

evals.prompt: I have a free weekend — I'm going to knock out all 60 submissions in one sitting. Good idea?
evals.expected_output: Should advise against it, citing Rule 4 (velocity pacing): a sudden spike of near-identical new links looks manufactured; drip 5-10 per week instead. Should also give the practical reasons: verification emails need same-day confirmation, per-platform quirks need logging, and a slow pace makes dofollow/live verification manageable. Should suggest using the weekend to lock the brand kit, do the Step 2 audit of existing listings, and prepare the tracker instead.
assertions: Advises against the one-day blast
Cites velocity pacing (5-10 per week)
Explains the manufactured-spike footprint concern
Gives practical batch-quality reasons
Redirects the effort to brand kit, audit, and tracker prep

evals.prompt: My site is online-only — no office, no storefront. Which directories can I actually use, and what do I put in the address fields?
evals.expected_output: Should identify the no-address path: Tier 1 identity profiles, Tier 3 content profiles, Tier 4 blog directories, Tier 5 bookmarking, and the no-address web directories (e.g., WebWiki, Curlie) all work without a street address. Should state that Tier 7 (Google Business Profile, local citations) is off-limits — never fake an address for GBP (suspension risk, poisons trust). Should address privacy: never put a home address on public listings; use the registered business or virtual-office address for directories that require one, or skip them. Should note the catalog marks which platforms require an address.
assertions: Identifies the no-address tiers and platforms
Forbids faking an address for Google Business Profile
Warns never to use a home address publicly
Offers registered/virtual address or skipping as options
Points to the address column in the catalog/tracker

evals.prompt: If I do all these directory submissions, how much will my rankings improve?
evals.expected_output: Should give the honest answer: individually these links are weak and mostly nofollow; directories alone won't move competitive rankings. Should explain the real value: a diverse, natural-looking base of referring domains, brand entity verification (Google + AI answer engines cross-reference Crunchbase/LinkedIn/major directories), owning brand-name search results, and a small amount of dofollow equity. Should frame them as the foundation layer that supports content and editorial links, and point to the KPI table (referring domains +30-50, link-type mix, listings verified) rather than promising ranking positions. Should warn that anyone promising rankings from directories alone is selling snake oil.
assertions: States individually weak / mostly nofollow honestly
Explains diversity and entity-verification value
Mentions AI answer engines cross-referencing major profiles
Frames directories as the foundation supporting content links
Points to KPI targets rather than promised rankings
Includes the snake-oil warning
